title: Gerunds and infinitives
lesson: Some verbs are followed by the -ing form (enjoy doing, finish doing), others by the to-infinitive (want to do, decide to do). A few verbs change meaning with each form: 'stop doing' means quit an activity, but 'stop to do' means pause in order to do something; 'remember/forget doing' looks back at the past, while 'remember/forget to do' looks forward to a duty. After a preposition, always use the gerund (good at swimming, interested in learning).

items.text: I really enjoy {1} long novels on rainy afternoons.
items.explanation: 'Enjoy' is always followed by the gerund (-ing form), never the infinitive.

items.text: She has decided {1} abroad for a year before university.
On the way home he stopped {1} a coffee at his favourite cafe.

blanks.hint: stop in order to do something

items.explanation: 'Stop to do' means to pause one activity in order to do something else; here he paused his journey in order to buy a coffee.

items.text: The doctor told him he should stop {1} if he wanted to feel healthier.
items.explanation: 'Stop doing' means to quit or give up an activity; he should give up the habit of smoking.

items.text: I distinctly remember {1} the door before we left the house.
items.explanation: 'Remember doing' refers to a memory of a past action — he recalls that he did lock the door.

items.text: Please remember {1} the report before you leave the office tonight.
items.explanation: 'Remember to do' refers to not forgetting a future task or duty.

items.text: I'll never forget {1} the Alps for the first time as a child.
items.explanation: 'Forget doing' refers to a past experience that one remembers (or, in the negative, will never forget).

items.text: He's very good at {1} problems that nobody else can solve.
items.explanation: After a preposition such as 'at', the verb must take the gerund form.

items.text: We're really looking forward to {1} you again next summer.
items.explanation: In 'look forward to', the word 'to' is a preposition, so it is followed by the gerund, not the bare infinitive.

items.text: They finally managed {1} the contract after months of negotiation.
items.explanation: 'Manage' (to succeed in doing something difficult) is followed by the to-infinitive.

items.text: She apologised for {1} late and promised it wouldn't happen again.
items.explanation: After the preposition 'for' (apologise for), the verb takes the gerund form.

items.text: I can't help {1} that something is wrong with the engine.
items.explanation: 'Can't help doing' is a fixed expression meaning to be unable to stop yourself; it is always followed by the gerund.

items.text: If the door is stuck, try {1} a little oil on the hinges.

blanks.hint: try = experiment with a possible solution

items.explanation: 'Try doing' means to experiment with something as a possible solution; here, putting oil on the hinges is one method that might solve the problem.

items.text: Taking on this new role will {1} working much longer hours than before.
items.explanation: After the modal 'will' the verb takes the base form 'involve'; 'involve' (meaning 'entail' or 'have as a consequence') is then followed by the gerund, which is why 'working' is in the -ing form.

items.text: After a few weeks she got used to {1} on the left side of the road.

blanks.hint: 'to' in 'be/get used to' is a preposition

items.explanation: In 'be/get used to' (meaning to become accustomed to), 'to' is a preposition, so it must be followed by the gerund, not the bare infinitive.

items.text: After a brief pause for questions, the lecturer went on {1} the second half of her argument.

blanks.hint: go on + to-infinitive = move to the next thing

items.explanation: 'Go on to do' means to do something next, after finishing something else; here the lecturer moved on to a new stage of her talk. (Compare 'go on doing', which means to continue the same activity.)

items.text: I regret {1} you that your application has not been successful.

blanks.hint: regret + to-infinitive for bad news you are about to give

items.explanation: 'Regret to do' (with verbs like inform, tell, say, announce) introduces bad news that the speaker is giving now and is sorry about. This contrasts with 'regret doing', which expresses sorrow about a past action.
